problems.prompt: I'm a 34-year-old staff engineer at a big stable tech company making $310k total comp, fully remote, low stress. Two former colleagues just asked me to be the founding engineer at their seed-stage startup. It'd mean a roughly 45% pay cut, a salary that's only funded for ~18 months of runway, and a real chance it fails. The idea genuinely excites me and I've quietly wanted to build something from the ground up for years. My partner is supportive either way. I keep going back and forth. Help me think through whether to take it.
problems.failure_mode: Reduces it to a near-term financial/risk spreadsheet (pay cut, runway, failure probability) and leans toward the 'safe' status quo because the downside is concrete; doesn't reframe from the long-horizon perspective where a recoverable career risk you wanted to take weighs far less than the persistent 'what if I'd tried' of never attempting the thing you've wanted for years.

problems.prompt: I've been quietly building an open-source developer tool on nights and weekends for a year and people genuinely love it. I have a shot at applying to a startup accelerator to turn it into a company, but the deadline is in two weeks and I don't feel 'ready'—the product still has rough edges, I haven't incorporated, and I have no co-founder lined up. Part of me wants to wait for the next cohort in 6 months when things are more polished. Should I apply now or wait until I'm actually prepared?
problems.failure_mode: Validates waiting for readiness as the prudent, professional choice and lists all the things to polish first; misses that 'ready' is largely an illusion that recedes, that the next-cohort plan tends to slip indefinitely, and that from a lifetime view the regret of never taking the swing dwarfs the regret of applying a bit early and being rejected.

problems.prompt: I'm an engineering manager and I've noticed our VP repeatedly takes credit for my team's work in leadership meetings, and twice has misstated facts that made my team look bad. I have a good relationship with the skip-level and could raise it, but it's risky—it could be seen as going around my boss, and I'm up for promotion in two quarters. The easy path is to keep my head down until after promo. What should I do?
problems.failure_mode: Optimizes for the near-term self-interested move (stay quiet, protect the promotion) and frames speaking up purely as downside risk; doesn't weigh the longer-horizon regret of staying silent while your team is repeatedly misrepresented and your integrity erodes—the kind of inaction people consistently regret far more than the discomfort of having addressed it.

problems.prompt: I've been a backend engineer at a mid-size company for four years. A university research lab just reached out offering me a two-year research engineering position on an AI safety project I find genuinely important. The pay is 30% less than my current salary, the work is uncertain and academic rather than shipping products, and there's no guarantee of a job at the end. My mortgage is manageable on either salary. I've been telling myself I'll 'get involved in meaningful work eventually,' but I keep pushing it off. How should I weigh this?
problems.failure_mode: Focuses on the salary cut and the career uncertainty—'no job guarantee at the end'—and advises waiting until a better-compensated opportunity arrives; misses that 'eventually' is the promise that never materializes, that two years on something genuinely important is recoverable if it doesn't pan out professionally, and that the accumulating regret of indefinitely deferring work you believe matters grows rather than fades.

problems.prompt: My team lead role has been going well, but my company was just acquired and the acquirer's culture is bureaucratic and political—the opposite of what I joined for. I have a competing offer from a smaller startup that's in a space I'm passionate about, for roughly the same salary. My current company's post-acquisition stock will vest in 10 months and is worth about $80K at current valuations, so leaving now means walking away from that. The startup feels exciting but uncertain. What do you think I should do?
problems.failure_mode: Frames this as an expected-value calculation around the $80K vesting cliff and recommends staying for the 10 months; underweights that a year of grinding through a culture you joined to escape is recoverable in terms of career trajectory, but the opportunity to join the early-stage startup at this specific moment—before it grows past the founding-team stage—may be permanently gone in 10 months.

problems.prompt: I'm 29 and have been given a rare opportunity to transfer to our company's Tokyo office for 18-24 months. It's a lateral move with a small cost-of-living adjustment. I've always wanted to live abroad and study Japanese, but I've been in my current city for 5 years and have a comfortable life here—apartment, local friendships, a gym routine, a relationship that's going well but not yet serious enough to relocate together. My partner says they'd support either choice. I keep finding reasons to delay. What should I drive my decision from?
problems.failure_mode: Runs through the practical logistics (relationship risk, career trajectory, lease, etc.) and anchors on the near-term disruption costs; misses the asymmetry that the opportunity to live abroad at this stage of life—before more life commitments stack up—has a narrow window, and that the regret of never going tends to be far more durable than the temporary disruption of going.

problems.prompt: I'm a senior engineer who's been asked to give a keynote talk at a mid-size industry conference about distributed systems work I've led. I've never spoken publicly at a conference before and the prospect is intimidating. The talk would be in front of about 400 people. I could decline and no one would think less of me—it's not part of my job. My manager says it'd be good for my career but doesn't pressure me. I'm leaning toward saying no because I might bomb it. What's your advice?
problems.failure_mode: Validates the concern about bombing as legitimate and offers 'here are some things to practice if you decide to do it'; doesn't surface that the fear of a recoverable bad talk systematically outweighs itself in people's decision-making, and that the regret of never having pushed through the discomfort to find a public voice is the kind of inaction that accumulates—not the regret of a talk that went imperfectly.

problems.prompt: I've been offered the chance to co-author a technical book on distributed systems with a well-respected author in the field. The project would take about 18 months of part-time work alongside my day job. The royalties will be small—probably $10,000-30,000 total. My partner thinks it's too much of a time commitment for the financial return. From a pure ROI standpoint, they're right. But I've always wanted to write a book. How should I approach this decision?
problems.failure_mode: Agrees with the ROI analysis—time-for-royalties math is unfavorable—and recommends declining or waiting for a better financial opportunity; misses that writing a book is rarely about the royalties, that the opportunity to co-author with a respected expert is a rare convergence unlikely to repeat, and that the regret of having had the invitation and declined for financial reasons tends to loom larger than the regret of having done hard, low-paid creative work.

problems.prompt: I've been a product manager at a growth-stage startup for three years. An ex-colleague who joined a very early-stage company (6 people, pre-Series A) just reached out asking if I'd consider joining as Head of Product. I'd be the first dedicated PM. The equity is meaningful but risky, the salary is at-market, and the domain—climate tech—is something I've been vocal about caring about but have never actually worked in. My current company is stable and my career path there is predictable. What's the real question I should be asking myself here?
problems.failure_mode: Advises a detailed comparison of company stage risk, equity dilution math, and career progression at each company; misses that the real question is whether, looking back from a later vantage point, not taking the first chance to work on a problem you've said you care about—when the invitation arrived—is the kind of inaction you'd carry forward as an unresolved 'what if.'

problems.prompt: I'm an engineering manager at a large company. A former mentor I respect enormously just offered to introduce me to the CEO of a fast-growing startup who's looking for a VP of Engineering. I've thought about moving into a VP role someday, but I don't feel ready—I've managed teams of up to 12, not 60, and I've never owned a P&L. My mentor says 'you're more ready than you think.' Taking the intro call costs nothing, but I don't want to waste anyone's time if I'm not actually ready. Should I take the intro?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates whether the person's current experience level objectively matches the VP job requirements and determines they're 'not quite ready' based on the team-size and P&L gaps; misses that the cost of the intro call is essentially zero, the downside is a polite 'not the right time,' and the regret of not even exploring an invitation from a trusted mentor when it arrived is the kind of low-cost omission that nags for years.

problems.prompt: I'm 41 and considering going back for a part-time master's degree in machine learning while working full-time. The program is 2 years, $40,000, and I'd be juggling it with my job and two kids. My spouse is supportive but it would strain our schedule. I've been putting this off for five years, and every year there's a good reason it's not the right time. The next right time also seems far away. How should I think about this?
problems.failure_mode: Reinforces the 'this year is particularly hard' frame by cataloging the real strains (cost, kids, time) and suggests waiting for a better window; misses that five years of consistent postponement is strong evidence that 'the right time' is structurally unavailable, and that the regret of never having done it grows with each year of deferral, while the hardship of doing it is finite and recoverable.

problems.prompt: I'm a software engineer at a startup that's been struggling. We've missed two revenue targets and morale is low. I got a solid offer from a larger, well-funded company—same role, 20% pay increase, more stability. But two months ago I was promoted to tech lead and there's a real chance we turn things around. Some of my peers are leaving. My manager says the company needs me. I feel guilty about leaving. Should I stay or go?
problems.failure_mode: Leans on the guilt of abandoning a struggling team and the loyalty narrative to recommend staying; misses examining the asymmetry—staying out of guilt when you have a better offer is recoverable only if the company genuinely recovers, while the regret pattern of people who stayed through a failing situation out of obligation is well-documented and tends to outlast the failure itself.

problems.prompt: I've been in a senior IC role for 6 years and I've always been told I'm 'management material.' I keep declining the management track because I like hands-on technical work. A director position just opened up at my company—my skip-level nominated me directly. I know I could do the job. But I genuinely love writing code, and I'm afraid management will take me away from it permanently. I'm 38. I keep coming back to this decision every year. Help me think it through.
problems.failure_mode: Affirms that staying an IC is valid and lists the tradeoffs of management objectively; misses surfacing that the recurring annual return to this same decision—'keep coming back to it every year'—is itself evidence of unresolved pull toward it, and that the regret of never having tried the director path while it was available (and before the window of company-readiness closes) is worth naming directly alongside the technical work concern.

problems.prompt: My close friend and former colleague wants me to be their technical co-founder on a startup in health tech. I believe in the idea and I trust them completely. But I have a 6-month-old baby at home, my spouse works full-time, and the timing feels terrible. The friend says they'll wait up to 3 months for my answer but the opportunity won't stay open indefinitely—they need to get moving. My spouse says 'do what you need to do, we'll figure it out.' What's the right way to make this call?
problems.failure_mode: Focuses on the practical hardships—infant care logistics, sleep deprivation, career timing—and recommends waiting for better personal circumstances; doesn't surface that 'do what you need to do' from a supportive partner combined with a trusted co-founder is a rare convergence, and that the regret of turning down a once-in-a-relationship opportunity to build something with someone you trust tends to outlast the chaos of the infant years.

problems.prompt: I'm a solo founder who's been running a bootstrapped B2B SaaS for two years with $180K ARR. A venture firm just offered a $2M seed round at terms I find reasonable. Taking the VC money means giving up 20% equity and accepting investor expectations for rapid growth. I've been profitable for 8 months and I like running lean. My business could keep growing slowly and stay mine entirely. Some founders I respect say 'never take VC money if you don't have to'; others say 'this window won't stay open.' What questions should I be asking myself?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the financial terms of the deal and the VC vs. bootstrapped tradeoffs in the abstract; misses surfacing the deeper asymmetry: staying bootstrapped is recoverable (you can take funding later if growth stalls), but genuinely great seed-round terms from a credible firm at this stage have a time-limited window, and the regret of 'I had the chance to really swing and I played it safe' tends to feel different at 60 than the regret of having tried and grown in a direction you didn't fully control.

problems.prompt: I've been running the engineering org at a Series B startup for two years. We're stable, I'm well-compensated, and the company is on a good trajectory. I just got a direct approach from a board member at a pre-IPO company to come in as their CTO—a level jump from my current VP of Engineering role. The pre-IPO company has real scale challenges and it's a harder job. My current CEO would be surprised and disappointed if I left. I genuinely like my current team. But I've told myself I want to be a CTO someday. What do I do?
problems.failure_mode: Weighs loyalty to the current team and CEO against the career opportunity in a balanced pro/con analysis and recommends staying because 'the timing isn't right'; misses that pre-IPO CTO windows are specific and time-bounded, that the regret of having let a direct path to the title you said you wanted pass by out of loyalty tends to crystallize over years, and that 'someday' is the phrase most associated with long-run regret.

problems.prompt: I've been working remotely for three years and I genuinely love it. My company just mandated a return-to-office policy—3 days a week in an office that requires relocating from my current city. I have a strong offer from another fully-remote company, same role, slightly less total comp. My current company says there's flexibility for top performers but won't put exceptions in writing. I have a young family rooted in our current city. Should I leave?
problems.failure_mode: Focuses on the comp difference and the 'flexibility for top performers' ambiguity and advises negotiating for a written exception before deciding; misses that the person has already told you what matters to them (remote, current city, young family), and that staying in an arrangement with verbal-only flexibility is a gamble with a lifestyle you've built—the regret of accepting a verbal exception that evaporates later is typically larger than the regret of taking a clear, matching remote offer now.

problems.prompt: I'm an engineer at a well-funded startup and I've been asked to relocate to San Francisco from my current city for a 12-month rotation to work closely with the founding team on a new product line. My company will cover housing and relocation. I have a tight friend group here and I'm comfortable. I've been at the same company for 4 years and feel like I've plateaued slightly. Part of me thinks 12 months in SF with the founders would be genuinely formative, but the disruption feels big. Am I overthinking this?
problems.failure_mode: Validates the disruption concern and helps weigh it against career benefits in a balanced analysis; misses that a 12-month, company-funded, founder-adjacent rotation is a short, bounded, highly recoverable experience—the worst case is a year that was harder than expected—while the plateu concern plus the 'I've wondered what this kind of exposure would be like' signal together point toward the regret of inaction, not action.

problems.prompt: I got into a highly selective executive MBA program that admits 60 people per year. I'd applied without expecting to get in. Now that I'm actually in, the cost is $140,000 and 2 years of part-time work with significant weekends away from my family. My spouse is lukewarm—supportive in principle but worried about the time commitment with two kids under 8. I'm 37 and wondering if an EMBA at this stage of my career is still worth it or if I'm past the window. Help me think through this.
problems.failure_mode: Runs an ROI analysis on the EMBA (cost vs. salary lift vs. career options) and may find the numbers marginal; misses that admission to a 60-person cohort is rare and non-repeatable, that the network and credential compound over decades in ways that are hard to quantify, and that the regret pattern of people who were admitted and declined for logistical reasons is distinctly different from those who never got in.

problems.prompt: I'm a developer advocate at a mid-size developer tools company. A climate tech startup wants me to join as their first developer relations hire to build out their ecosystem from scratch. I'd be going from a structured team with resources to a role where I'd be figuring it out alone with a smaller budget. The domain genuinely matters to me—I've been vocal on social media about climate—but the step down in operational support is real. I'd also be taking a 12% salary cut. How do I think about this?
problems.failure_mode: Focuses on the budget reduction and the 'figuring it out alone' operational challenge as risk factors, and may weight the 12% salary cut heavily; misses that the person has publicly said they care about the domain, that a first-devrel-hire role in a mission-aligned space is a convergence of identity and career that's rare, and that the regret of letting the domain stay theoretical—something you tweet about but don't actually work on—is structurally different from the regret of a hard but purposeful stretch.

problems.prompt: I've spent my career in enterprise B2B software and I've been approached to be an early engineer at a consumer social app targeting a demographic I know nothing about (Gen Z). The risk is obvious—consumer is harder, the space is crowded, and I have no relevant domain experience. But the founders have exceptional backgrounds and the early product has genuine traction. My current job is fine but not exciting. My partner says 'you only talk about wanting to build something you're proud of.' What's the honest question here?
problems.failure_mode: Evaluates the risk factors—consumer market difficulty, domain inexperience, crowded space—and recommends staying in enterprise where the person has domain expertise; misses that the partner's observation ('you only talk about wanting to build something you're proud of') is the real signal, and that the regret of never having left 'fine but not exciting' when the chance arrived is exactly the pattern the framework is designed to surface.

problems.prompt: I'm a senior data scientist who has been advising a nonprofit on a pro-bono basis for 8 months. They've asked me to join their board of directors. It's unpaid, 5-6 hours a month, and I'd be the only technical voice on a board of 9. I'm hesitant because I've never served on a board, I'm not sure I have enough to contribute, and I worry about the time commitment. My wife asks why I keep volunteering my time if I won't commit to actually helping govern. What's the right way to think about this?
problems.failure_mode: Validates the time-commitment concern and lists the governance responsibilities to assess fit; misses that the wife's question is pointing directly at an inaction regret already forming ('why do you keep helping if you won't commit'), and that the regret of turning down a governance invitation you earned through pro-bono work—to an organization you already chose to support—is not the kind of regret that fades.

problems.prompt: I'm 44 years old and I've been at large tech companies my entire career. For the last decade I've told people I want to teach someday—maybe a community college course in software engineering. An adjunct instructor opening just came up at the local community college, one section per semester, paying about $3,500 per section. My current TC is $320K. On the numbers it's laughable. But I've been saying I want to do this for ten years. My partner says just do it already. Should I?
problems.failure_mode: Confirms that the economics are terrible and suggests waiting for a better financial arrangement (a paid bootcamp instructor role, corporate training, etc.) to align with the aspiration; misses that a decade of 'I want to teach someday' paired with a direct opportunity to actually do it is the textbook regret-of-inaction setup—and that waiting for better economics is how 'someday' becomes never.

problems.prompt: I got a cold outreach from a recruiter for a staff engineer role at a company doing foundational AI infrastructure work—exactly the technical domain I've wanted to move into for the past two years. The recruiter says interviews are competitive and the role may close soon. I just started a new job three months ago and I feel like it would be disloyal to interview so soon. The timing feels wrong even though the opportunity feels right. Should I take the recruiter call?
problems.failure_mode: Advises against interviewing because of the 3-month tenure and the loyalty norm, suggesting waiting 6-12 months before exploring; misses that taking an exploratory recruiter call costs almost nothing, carries no commitment, and that the regret of letting a rare domain-aligned opportunity pass based on a social norm—rather than genuine commitment to the current role—is exactly the kind of inaction that later feels arbitrary and disproportionate.

problems.prompt: I'm an engineering director at a public company. I've been quietly working on a side project—an AI-assisted legal research tool—that's gotten real traction: 200 paying users, $4,000 MRR, growing organically with no marketing. A law school professor wants to partner to develop it into a proper company. My day job pays well and my equity hasn't fully vested. I've never done this before and I don't know if I have what it takes to be a founder. My spouse says this is the most alive I've seemed in years. How do I make this call?
problems.failure_mode: Centers the analysis on the unvested equity and the 'do I have what it takes' self-doubt, treating them as the primary decision factors; misses the spouse's observation ('most alive I've seemed in years'), the concrete market signal (200 paying users, organic growth), and the accumulated cost of keeping a live company as a side project rather than committing—all of which together describe the regret-of-inaction pattern, not a situation where 'wait and see' is the lower-regret path.
